coredata.dc:title: Hyaluronic acid-based M1 macrophage targeting and environmental responsive drug releasing nanoparticle for enhanced treatment of rheumatoid arthritis

coredata.dc:description: Herein, hyaluronic acid (HA) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is used to form targeted drug delivery platform HCPC/DEX NPs with previously prepared carbon dots (CDs) as cross-linker, dexamethasone (DEX) is loaded for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) treatment. The drug loading capacity of β-CD and M1 macrophage targeting of HA were utilized for efficient delivery of DEX to the inflammatory joints. Because of the environmental responsive degradation of HA, DEX can be released in 24 h and inhibit the inflammatory response in M1 macrophages. The drug loading of NPs is 4.79 %. Cellular uptake evaluation confirmed that NPs can specifically target to M1 macrophages via HA ligands, the uptake of M1 macrophages is 3.7 times that of normal macrophages. In vivo experiments revealed that NPs can accumulate in RA joints to alleviate inflammation and accelerate cartilage healing, the accumulation can be observed in 24 h. The cartilage thickness increased to 0.45 mm after HCPC/DEX NPs treatment, indicating its good RA therapeutic effect. Importantly, this study was the first to utilize the potential acid and reactive oxygen species responsiveness of HA to release drug and prepare M1 macrophage targeting nanodrug for RA treatment, which provides a safe and effective RA therapeutic strategy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of products. (a) 1H NMR spectra (600 MHz), (b) FTIR spectra, (c) TEM images, (d) size distribution (measured by Nano Zetasizer ZS 90) and zeta potential, (e) FL emission spectra (under 420 nm excitation) of products. (f) FL emission spectra of HCPC. (g) Photographs of DEX in CPC solution or deionized water. Data are mean ± SD, n = 3.

In vitro properties of HCPC NPs. Effects of mass ratio of (a) CPC/DEX and (b) CPC/HA on DL and EE of HCPC/DEX NPs. (c) Particle size and (d) PDI of HCPC/DEX NPs incubating under 4 °C or 37 °C. In vitro drug release behavior of (e) free DEX, CPC/DEX, and HCPC/DEX NPs under pH 7.4 and (f) HCPC/DEX NPs under different conditions. (g) TEM images of CPC/DEX NPs and HCPC/DEX NPs incubated under pH 5.0 + H2O2 condition. (h) In vitro drug release behavior of HCPC/DEX NPs in different concentration of H2O2. (i) Hemolysis ratio of HCPC (inset is the representative images of hemolysis test). Data are mean ± SD, n = 3.

Cell experiments of HCPC NPs. Cell viability after incubation with (a) HCPC and (b) HCPC/DEX for 48 h. (c) The expression of CD86 in normal and LPS induced RAW 264.7 cells. Effects of HCPC/DEX NPs on (d) TNF-α, (e) IL-1β, and (f) IL-6 secreted from LPS induced macrophages. Normal and LPS induced macrophages were used as negative and positive control groups. (g) Fluorescence images of cells incubated with 200 μg/mL of HCPC for 12 h. (h) Cellular distribution of HCPC in Caco-2 cells. (i) Intracellular drug release of HCPC/IR780 NPs in C28/I2 cells. (j) Typical cellular uptake images and (k) relative fluorescence intensity of CPC and HCPC to macrophages. Different-state macrophages were incubated with CPC or HCPC at a concentration of 100 μg/mL for 2 h. Scale bars for all images are 50 μm. Data are mean ± SD, n = 3, ⁎ P < 0.05, ⁎⁎ P < 0.01.

In vivo biodistribution and therapeutic effects of HCPC NPs in AA rats. (a) Schematic diagram of AA rat model establishment and therapeutic treatment. (b) In vivo biodistribution of free IR780, CPC/IR780, and HCPC/IR780 NPs. (c) Ex vivo images of HCPC/IR780 NPs in main organs and hind paws. (d) Body weight, (e) AI, and (f) paw thickness of rats with different treatments (inset is the representative hind paw images at Day 30). The expression of (g) TNF-α, (h) IL-1β, and (i) IL-6 in synovial tissue of ankle joints. Data are mean ± SD, n = 6, ⁎ P < 0.05, ⁎⁎ P < 0.01 vs. AA group.

Micro-CT analysis of the hind paws. (a) The representative visual 3D images of ankle joints (yellow arrows indicate osteophyte and hyperplasia, green arrows show bone destruction, red arrows show trabecular osteopenia, blue arrows indicate subchondral bone sclerosis). Quantitative results of bone histomorphometry parameters including (b) BV/TV, (c) Tb.Th, (d) Tb.N, and (e) BMD. Data are mean ± SD, n = 6, ⁎ P < 0.05, ⁎⁎ P < 0.01 vs. AA group.

Histological analysis of ankle joint tissues. Representative images of (a) H&E and (b) SOFG staining. (c) Cartilage thickness and (d) histology score for the degree of cellular infiltration. Data are mean ± SD, n = 6, ⁎ P < 0.05, ⁎⁎ P < 0.01 vs. AA group.

Biosafety of HCPC/DEX NPs in normal rats. (a) H&E stained histological sections of major organs. Scale bars for all images are 100 μm. (b) Effects of HCPC/DEX NPs on the organ indexes. Data are mean ± SD, n = 6.
Schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis of HCPC NPs and its applications in enhanced treatment of RA via passive targeting, M1 macrophage targeting, and responsive drug release.

head.ce:title: Hyaluronic acid-based M1 macrophage targeting and environmental responsive drug releasing nanoparticle for enhanced treatment of rheumatoid arthritis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, hyaluronic acid (HA) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is used to form targeted drug delivery platform HCPC/DEX NPs with previously prepared carbon dots (CDs) as cross-linker, dexamethasone (DEX) is loaded for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) treatment. The drug loading capacity of β-CD and M1 macrophage targeting of HA were utilized for efficient delivery of DEX to the inflammatory joints. Because of the environmental responsive degradation of HA, DEX can be released in 24 h and inhibit the inflammatory response in M1 macrophages. The drug loading of NPs is 4.79 %. Cellular uptake evaluation confirmed that NPs can specifically target to M1 macrophages via HA ligands, the uptake of M1 macrophages is 3.7 times that of normal macrophages. In vivo experiments revealed that NPs can accumulate in RA joints to alleviate inflammation and accelerate cartilage healing, the accumulation can be observed in 24 h. The cartilage thickness increased to 0.45 mm after HCPC/DEX NPs treatment, indicating its good RA therapeutic effect. Importantly, this study was the first to utilize the potential acid and reactive oxygen species responsiveness of HA to release drug and prepare M1 macrophage targeting nanodrug for RA treatment, which provides a safe and effective RA therapeutic strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by persistent synovitis, bone erosion, and cartilage damage in the joint, which will eventually lead to long-term pain and even disability (Gravallese, 2002; Schett, 2019). At present, the most commonly used method for RA is still drug therapy, among which glucocorticoids (GCs) are widely used immunosuppressants in inflammatory diseases and autoimmune diseases. GCs can alleviate joint swelling and cartilage injury effectively, and inhibit the M1 polarization of macrophages in RA joints. Dexamethasone (DEX) has been used as an anti-inflammatory drug of RA for >70 years (Bijlsma & Jacobs, 2014; Conn, 2021). For RA treatment, a sufficient dose of DEX is required at the inflammatory site to produce an anti-inflammatory effect. However, DEX lacks organ/tissue targeting ability after oral or systemic administration, so higher dose and frequency are required to achieve effective concentration at the inflammation site (Ni et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2020). Chronic administration of high-dose DEX may lead to serious side effects on the hematological system and alimentary system (Black & Grodzinsky, 2019; Malkawi et al., 2018), even resulting in osteonecrosis and osteoporosis (Jiang et al., 2019; K. Xu et al., 2021), which greatly limit the use of DEX in RA.

Hyaluronic acid (HA) is also a treatment for RA. HA is an endogenous mucopolysaccharide, which widely exists in the human body. It has good biocompatibility, biodegradability, and hydrophilicity, and has many biological activities such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and cell proliferation regulation (Vasvani, Kulkarni, & Rawtani, 2020). Therefore, it is widely used in biomaterials, drug targeted delivery systems, medical cosmetology, and postoperative adhesion prevention (Hou et al., 2022; Zhai et al., 2020). HA is also a component of cartilage extracellular matrix. In normal joints, synovial cells secret HA into joint cavity, producing functions such as lubricating joints, scavenging ROS, relieving inflammation, and promoting chondrocyte proliferation. In RA joints, most HA degrades and the arthroprotective effect decreases. Intra-articular injection of HA is considered an effective treatment for RA (Campo et al., 2012; Sato et al., 1988). However, HA is rapidly cleared through the subsynovial lymphatic system several hours after injection, so repeated injections are required to achieve therapeutic effects (Adams, Lussier, & Peyron, 2000). But frequent administration may lead to problems such as nerve injury, increased synovial inflammation, and severe bacterial infections, which affect the therapeutic effect of HA (Adams et al., 2000). Therefore, combining the characteristics of RA joints and HA, it is important to develop a novel drug delivery system that can target DEX and HA to inflammatory joints through intravenous injection to improve the therapeutic effect of RA and reduce adverse reactions (Jeong & Park, 2021; Song et al., 2021).

The formation of synovial pannus in RA will seriously affect the joint structure and function, leading to joint deformity and failure. However, the distorted internal structure of the lesion pannus and the enhanced vascular permeability will cause extravasation through leaky vasculature and subsequent inflammatory cell-mediated sequestration (ELVIS) effect (Yuan, Quan, Cui, Goldring, & Wang, 2012). Nanoparticles (NPs) with appropriate particle size will accumulate through the ELVIS effect in inflammatory joints and provide enhanced therapeutic effect (Pan et al., 2022).

In addition, it has been proved that the infiltration of inflammatory cells is one of the main factors causing cartilage damage in RA patients (Deng et al., 2021; Jang, Kwon, & Lee, 2022; Rana, Li, Dang, & Yang, 2018), especially M1 macrophages (Li, Hsu, & Mountz, 2012; Udalova, Mantovani, & Feldmann, 2016; Wang et al., 2017). Macrophages are important immune cells derived from progenitor cells in the bone marrow. Macrophages are widely distributed in the whole body, such as osteoclasts in bone tissue and synovial macrophages in joints (Hannemann, Apparailly, & Courties, 2021; Hasegawa & Ishii, 2022). They are rich in lysosomal granules and related enzymes, and can phagocytize and clear the antigenic foreign bodies (Ross, Devitt, & Johnson, 2021). In RA inflamed joints, the overexpressed cytokines such as granulocyte macrophage colony stimulating factor (GM-CSF) and interferon-γ (IFN-γ) can recruit macrophages and promote the M1 polarization of macrophages (Adamopoulos et al., 2006; Kim et al., 2020). M1 macrophages can participate and promote inflammatory reactions by secreting proinflammatory cytokines and other inflammatory substances such as tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-1β (IL-1β), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Grabiec, Korchynskyi, Tak, & Reedquist, 2012; Meniailo et al., 2018; Tarique et al., 2015). Articular cartilage is a kind of nonvascular tissue and its self-healing ability is limited (Santhagunam, Madeira, & Cabral, 2012). There is increasing evidence that persistent low-grade inflammation can exacerbate cartilage injury, in which M1 macrophages play a crucial role (Griffin & Scanzello, 2019). One of the important anti inflammatory mechanisms of DEX is the inhibition of macrophage M1 polarization. Therefore, targeted delivery of DEX to M1 macrophages will be helpful to RA therapy. For example, modifying NPs with HA can enhance the ligand-receptor interaction with CD44 receptors overexpressed on the surface of M1 macrophages, and improve the active targeting ability of NPs to macrophages in inflammatory sites of RA joints (Montanari, Di Meo, Oates, Coviello, & Matricardi, 2018; Qadri, Almadani, Jay, & Elsaid, 2018; Wang et al., 2020).

Another important feature of RA is the increased ROS in the synovium of inflamed joints (Smallwood et al., 2018). In inflammatory site, macrophages are recruited and activated into M1 type by GM-CSF and IFN-γ, and ROS is released abundantly (Mitchell, Moots, & Wright, 2017). The increase of ROS will activate metalloproteinase, inhibit the synthesis of cartilage proteoglycan, promote chondrocyte apoptosis, and finally lead to the destruction of cartilage and surrounding tissues (Hadjigogos, 2003; Henrotin, Bruckner, & Pujol, 2003). Furthermore, excess ROS will also lead to the oxidative degradation of HA (Bastow et al., 2008), the rapid reduction of HA makes articular cartilage vulnerable to wear and further aggravates RA (Zhang et al., 2021). The excessive ROS will also increase the level of proinflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, in inflammatory joints and further recruit and activate macrophages into M1 type. Which will form an inflammation cascade reaction, the inflammation will be constantly amplified, resulting in a vicious circle (Z. Chen et al., 2019; Lepetsos, Papavassiliou, & Papavassiliou, 2019; W. Li et al., 2021; Mirshafiey & Mohsenzadegan, 2008). Based on the increased ROS in RA articular cavity, preparing ROS responsive NPs and selectively releasing drug to inflammatory sites is of great significance for RA treatment. HA is considered to be oxidized and hydrolyzed by ROS, which makes it a potential strategy for the preparation of ROS responsive NPs (Hu et al., 2020). The excess ROS in RA joints leads to rapid dissociation of NPs and responsive release of drug at inflammatory sites. So that ROS responsive NPs can effectively improve the therapeutic effect of RA (Fan et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2021).

In our previous study (Wang et al., 2021), a kind of PEI modified carbon dots (CDs) with high fluorescence was synthesized. The sufficient surface amino groups, good water solubility, and biocompatibility of the CDs are considered beneficial for preparing CDs-based NPs for RA treatment. And because of the good fluorescence performance, CDs can be conducive to monitoring the distribution of NPs and observing the process of drug releasing from NPs. However, the low drug loading efficiency of CDs limits its application for DEX delivery and chemical bonding with DEX may affect drug activity. To facilitate the DEX loading, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) can be attached on the surface of CDs through the functional groups on CDs. β-CD is a cyclic oligosaccharide with a hydrophilic outer shell and a hollow cylindrical hydrophobic inner cavity. It can load hydrophobic drug of a certain size in the cavity, forming a host-guest inclusion complex, thus changing properties of the drug (Zhang et al., 2019). β-CD is biocompatible and will eventually hydrolyze into glucose in the body, so it shows good safety and is widely used in biomedicine and other fields (Zhang, Sun, & Jiang, 2018). However, β-CD inclusion complex also has problems such as indifferent sustained drug release effect and limited water solubility (Loftsson & Brewster, 1996). Therefore, combining β-CD with CDs to load drug and wrapping with HA to form drug delivery platform can overcome the above problems.

According to the pathological features of RA, in this study, multifunctional drug delivery platform based on HA and β-CD is designed for diagnosis and enhanced treatment of adjuvant-induced arthritis (AA) in rats (as shown in Scheme 1 ). HA is an active targeting ligand for M1 macrophages and the HA-modified NPs will be internalized into cells mainly via clathrin-mediated endocytosis and delivered to lysosomes (Lin & Lee, 2018). HA is proved to degrade under the action of acid and H2O2 (a kind of ROS), this is also used as a preparation method of low molecular weight hyaluronic acid (LMWHA) (Chen, Qin, & Hu, 2019; Choi et al., 2019). Inspired by the preparation process of LMWHA, the degradation condition of acid and ROS is consistent with the environment of M1 macrophage lysosome, so HA can be helpful in environmental responsive drug release in inflammatory sites. At present, no other reports have been searched that utilize the potential acid and ROS responsive degradation of HA to form drug delivery platform for responsive drug release in M1 macrophages. Therefore, this study for the first time improves the responsive DEX release with the help of the degrading characteristics of HA for RA treatment. Besides, HA can increase the uptake of M1 macrophages through the CD44-mediated endocytosis pathway, and improve the drug targeting of M1 macrophages, which expands the application of HA in drug delivery platforms. In addition, CDs is rich in functional groups and it can emit blue-green fluorescence, the abundant amino groups on CDs are conducive to drug loading and cross-linking with HA. Such a cross-linking between HA and CDs will further prevent premature drug leakage from β-CD. The β-CD can not only load drug for RA treatment, but also load lipophilic dye IR780 to form a dual fluorescence imaging system with CDs for dynamic observation of the distribution and drug release process in cells. Based on this, HA-CDs-PEG-β-CD/DEX nanoparticles (HCPC/DEX NPs) is prepared from HA, CDs, polyethylene glycol (PEG), and β-CD. The fluorescence properties, drug release profiles, stability studies, cytotoxicity evaluation, hemolysis test, cellular uptake, and distribution, in vitro anti-inflammatory effects, biodistribution, in vivo biosafety, and therapeutic effects of HCPC/DEX NPs on RA are studied in detail. Based on the increased M1 macrophages and ROS in RA joints, HCPC/DEX NPs is expected to improve the therapeutic efficacy of DEX on experimental RA in rats through M1 macrophage targeting and environmental responsive drug release.

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), HA (molecular weight 30,000-50,000), lipopolysaccharide (LPS), DEX, and complete Freund's adjuvant (CFA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (St Louis, MO, USA). NHS-PEG10-maleimide (NHS-PEG10-MAL) was obtained from Biomatrik Inc. (Zhejiang China). Mono(6-deoxy-6-mercapto)-β-cyclodextrin (SH-β-CD) was purchased from Innochem Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW = 1800), citric acid, and IR780 were obtained from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Raw 264.7 macrophages and Caco-2 colon cancer cells are kindly provided by Stem Cell Bank, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). C28/I2 human normal chondrocytes were provided by the First Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University (Shaanxi, China). The cell experiment protocols were performed in strict accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory cells of Xi'an Jiaotong University and were approved by the Ethics Committee of Xi'an Jiaotong University.

Male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (200–240 g) were obtained from the Laboratory Animal Center of Xi'an Jiaotong University, SCXK 2018–001 (Shaanxi, China). Animals were housed under a 12 h light-dark cycle at a constant ambient temperature (22–25 °C), with normal chow and water ad libitum. They were allowed to acclimatize for one week before the experiments were started.

This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Xi'an Jiaotong University (No. XJTULAC2016-004). All experimental procedures followed the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals.

The synthesis method of CDs is consistent with the previous literature (Wang et al., 2021). The preparation of HCPC/DEX NPs is shown in Figs. S1-S3. First, NHS-PEG10-MAL was added into CDs solution under pH 7.4 to obtain CDs-PEG-MAL (CP). After that, SH-β-CD was reacted with CP through thiol-ene click-chemistry reaction to obtain CDs-PEG-β-CD (CPC). Then DEX was dissolved in absolute alcohol and added into CPC under 40 °C to form CPC/DEX inclusion complex. Finally, HA was reacted with CPC/DEX to prepare HCPC/DEX NPs. Replace DEX with IR780 to prepare HCPC/IR780 NPs in the same way. The detailed methods for NPs preparation were described in supplementary information.

Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR, Avance III HD 600 MHz spectrometer) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer) were applied to confirm the special surface functional groups of products. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-F200 field emission transmission electron microscope) was used to examine the morphology and particle sizes of products. The particle sizes and zeta potential were further identified via Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. And the fluorescence (FL) properties were determined via F-2700 Spectrofluorophotometer.

10 mg HCPC/DEX were dispersed in 9.9 mL ethanol, 0.1 mL of HCl was added. The mixture was stirred at 50 °C and ultrasounded to disintegrate the NPs structure. The mixture was centrifuged at 13,000 ×g and the supernatant was measured by HPLC. The establishment of HPLC method for DEX detection was described in supplementary information. Drug loading (DL) and entrapment efficiency (EE) were calculated using the following equations:
EE
%
=
M dd
/
M d
× 100 %
DL
%
=
M dd
/
M n
× 100 %
where M
dd is the detected content of DEX, M
d is the DEX added in the system, and M
n is the total mass of NPs.

The in vitro drug release was investigated in PBS (pH 7.4, with 0.5 % Tween-80) using a dialysis method. In this study, DEX, CPC/DEX, and HCPC/DEX (equivalent to 100 μg of DEX) were placed in dialysis bags (molecular weight cut-off 2000 Da) and immersed in 30 mL of release medium for 72 h. The dialysis bags were incubated in a constant temperature shaking table, the temperature is 37 °C and the shaking speed is 100 r/min. At designated time points, 1 mL of release medium was sampled and replaced with fresh medium. Samples were detected by HPLC, the cumulative drug release (%) was calculated as follows:
Drug release
%
=
M t
/
M 0
× 100 %
where M
t is the cumulative release amount of drug at time t, M 0 is the initial amount of loaded drug.
In addition, drug release from HCPC/DEX in the presence of 1 mmol/L H2O2 or acidic condition was also carried out.

The stability study was conducted by detecting the particle sizes or polydispersity index (PDI) of HCPC/DEX NPs by Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Firstly, the in vitro stability of HCPC/DEX NPs solution was investigated by incubating at 4 °C or 37 °C for seven days. After that, lyophilized HCPC/DEX NPs sample was stored at room temperature or 4 °C for 90 d, a small amount of lyophilized sample was dissolved and the particle size and PDI were detected to study the storage stability of lyophilized HCPC/DEX NPs sample. To study the effects of hemodilution on NPs after intravenous injection, HCPC/DEX solution was diluted with deionized water for dilution stability evaluation. Finally, HCPC/DEX NPs solution was incubated with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C for 72 h to study the effects of protein adsorption on the stability of HCPC/DEX in blood.

RAW 264.7, Caco-2 and C28/I2 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin and maintained under a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. Cytotoxicity of HCPC was evaluated by MTT assay and the relative cell viability (%) was calculated as follows:
Cell viability
%
=
OD t
−
OD b
/
OD c
−
OD b
× 100 %
where OD
t is the absorbance of HCPC treated group, OD
b is the absorbance of blank group, OD
c is the absorbance of the control group.

Blood of SD rats was collected for hemolysis test, 0.2 % Triton X-100 and saline were used as positive and negative control. The hemolytic ratio (%) was calculated as follows:
Hemolytic ratio
%
=
OD s
−
OD n
/
OD p
−
OD n
× 100 %
where OD
s is the absorbance of sample, OD
n is the absorbance of negative control, OD
p is the absorbance of positive control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and effects on the expression of proinflammatory cytokines
ce:section.ce:para: Caco-2, RAW 264.7, and C28/I2 cells were induced by 1 μg/mL of LPS to simulate cells at inflammatory sites. After culturing overnight, 200 μg/mL of HCPC was incubated with cells for 12 h. Then the medium was removed and the cells were washed 3 times with cold PBS, fluorescence images were observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

To observe the M1 macrophage targeting of HCPC, 5 μg/mL of anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody (CD44 mAb) was incubated with LPS induced macrophages to obtain CD44 receptor blocked macrophages. Then 100 μg/mL of HCPC or CPC were incubated with normal, LPS induced, and CD44 receptor blocked macrophages for 2 h, after washing cells for 3 times, the fluorescence images were observed by fluorescence microscope.

To study the cellular distribution of HCPC, 200 μg/mL of HCPC was incubated with Caco-2 cells for 12 h. After fixing with 4 % paraformaldehyde, 4′-6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was added to stain the nuclei. The fluorescence images were observed by CLSM.

The process of intracellular drug release from NPs could be simulated by treating LPS induced C28/I2 cells with HCPC/IR780. 100 μg/mL of HCPC/IR780 was incubated with cells, and the fluorescence images were observed by CLSM.

The effects of HCPC/DEX on the expression of proinflammatory cytokines were evaluated by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method. Typically, RAW 264.7 cells were induced by LPS and treated with HCPC/DEX (equivalent to 5 μg/mL of DEX). After that, the cytokine concentrations in the cell culture supernatant were determined according to ELISA kit instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the adjuvant-induced arthritis rat model
ce:section.ce:para: The SD rats were injected intradermally at the tail base with 100 μL CFA (M. tuberculosis H-37Ra 1 mg/mL) on Day 0. On Day 7, rats were given booster immunization of 100 μL CFA (Xu et al., 2020). Then the therapeutic agents were given via tail vein injection every three days from Day 15. The progress of joint inflammation and swelling was monitored every other day.

Normal or AA rats were injected intravenously with free IR780, CPC/IR780, or HCPC/IR780 solutions (equivalent to 200 μg/kg of IR780, n = 3). Meanwhile, saline was injected to control rats. At 2, 6, and 24 h after administration, rats were anesthetized and the rats were observed under IVIS Spectrum living imaging system, the excitation wavelength is 745 nm and the detection wavelength is 820 nm. Then the rats were sacrificed immediately and the major organs were collected for ex vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic efficacy evaluation in AA rats
ce:section.ce:para: AA rats were randomly divided into four groups (n = 6), which were injected intravenously with saline, free DEX, CPC/DEX, and HCPC/DEX (equivalent to 1 mg/kg of DEX) on Day 15, 18, 21, 24, and 27 after the first immunization, respectively. Six untreated normal rats were served as control group. Subsequently, the paw thickness, arthritis index (AI), and body weight were measured every other day. The AI score is the sum of four paws (Ni et al., 2020), scoring standards was described in supplementary information. All data were measured and recorded by two independent experimenters.

On Day 30, animals were sacrificed and the hind paw ankle joints were dissected from rats. After fixing in 4 % paraformaldehyde, the ankle joints were scanned by Zeiss Xradia520 Versa micro-CT system. The visual 3D images were reconstructed through VGStudio MAX 3.0 software. To obtain quantitative results of different groups, choosing a 1.2 × 1.2 × 1.2 mm trabecular region of the distal end of tibia as the region of interest for micro-CT analysis. The morphometric parameters including bone volume fraction (BV/TV), trabecular number (Tb.N), trabecular thickness (Tb.Th), and bone mineral density (BMD) were calculated by Mimics and ABAQUS software.

For histological analysis, dissected hind paws were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde and decalcified completely in 10 % neutral EDTA solution. Decalcified hind paws were embedded in paraffin. Sections (4 μm) were stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and SafraninO-Fast Green (SOFG) according to standard methods.

On Day 30, blood and synovium were also collected from sacrificed rats. Serum and synovial tissue homogenate were prepared to determine the proinflammatory cytokine levels of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 through ELISA method.

Normal rats were injected intravenously with HCPC/DEX (equivalent to 1 mg/kg of DEX) every 3 days for safety evaluation. Animals were sacrificed and the major organs and serum were collected after the fifth administration. The alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST) creatinine (Cr), and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) in serum were measured through assay kits. The organ indexes and histological sections were also used for safety evaluation.

Statistical analyses are performed with SPSS Version 20.0 (IBM Corporation, USA). All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Fisher's least significant difference test is performed. P < 0.05 is considered to be statistically significant.

CDs showed good water solubility and fluorescence properties, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength were 420 nm and 480 nm. The sufficient amino groups derived from PEI are conducive to drug loading and cross-linking with HA to form NPs.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the structures of products were confirmed by 1H NMR. The proton peaks at 0.92–0.73 ppm belong to methyl protons in aliphatic saturated structures, peaks at 1.50–0.96 ppm are assigned to methylene protons in aliphatic saturated structures, peak at 1.82 ppm is associated with methyl or methylene protons adjacent to amino group, peaks at 2.25–1.84 ppm are consistent with methyl or methylene protons adjacent to CC or CO, peaks at 3.18–3.04 ppm correspond to methyl or methylene protons adjacent to amide bond. The amino group of CDs reacted to form amide bond, as a result, the peaks shift from 1.82 ppm to 3.18–3.04 ppm in CP and CPC. The peaks at 3.78–3.52 ppm are the characteristic peaks of PEG methylene, the increase of it in CP shows the formation of CDs-PEG. The peaks at 3.80 ppm and 5.03–4.97 ppm are characteristic peaks of β-CD, and the peaks at 4.09–3.99 ppm are attributed to methylene protons adjacent to -SH, the appearance of these peaks proves that SH-β-CD has been successfully connected with CP.

The products were also characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1b, the band at 3426 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H, the band at 2971 cm−1 is assigned to the asymmetric vibration of CH, the band at 1709 cm−1 is consistent with the stretching vibration of CO or CC, the band at 1563 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of NH, the band at 1412 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CN, the band at 1265 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of aromatic CO bonds, there is no aromatic structure in HA, while aromatic structure can be formed during the preparation of CDs. The appearance of band at 1265 cm−1 in HCPC proves that CPC has been successfully combined with HA. Meanwhile, the FTIR spectrum of HCPC is similar to HA in shape, indicating that there are HA residues on the surface of HCPC. The band at 1098 cm−1 comes from the C-O-C stretching vibration. There is no ether bond in CDs, while PEG and β-CD contain numbers of ether bonds, the band at 1098 cm−1 appears in CP and further increases in CPC, demonstrating the formation of CP and CPC. In addition, the FTIR spectra of CPC/DEX NPs and HCPC/DEX NPs are similar to CPC and HCPC, this phenomenon may be because DEX is loaded into the inner cavity of β-CD.

As shown in Fig. 1c, the morphology and particle size of products were identified by transmission electron microscope (TEM). CDs, CP, and CPC are uniform nanospheres with an average diameter of <10 nm, and their particle size increase gradually, suggesting that PEG and β-CD have been modified on CDs. Moreover, the particle size of CPC/DEX also increased, and their structure appeared to be denser, which may be attributed to the effective binding between DEX and CPC. The particle size of HCPC increased to >50 nm, which proves that HA is cross-linked with CPC. The intermolecular cross-linking compressed HA into spheres around 100 nm, and the HCPC/DEX showed similar structure, which is conducive to the passive targeting of inflammatory joints through the ELVIS effect.

The particle size was also measured through dynamic light scattering (DLS) method, as shown in Fig. 1d, the particle size of CDs, CP, CPC, CPC/DEX, HCPC, and HCPC/DEX are 5.95 ± 0.75 nm, 8.26 ± 1.18 nm, 9.24 ± 1.41 nm, 9.68 ± 1.60 nm, 82.59 ± 16.86 nm, and 89.73 ± 18.89 nm, respectively. The results of DLS are similar to TEM. The zeta potential of products was also measured by Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Because of the considerable amino groups, CDs has strong positive electricity. After the addition of negatively charged PEG and β-CD, the zeta potential of CP and CPC decreased from 20.90 ± 4.09 mV to 9.98 ± 4.03 mV and 6.23 ± 7.35 mV. HCPC was formed by cross-linking between HA and CPC so that carboxyl groups from HA remained on the surface and shielded the positive charge of amino groups. As a result, the zeta potential of HCPC reversed to −12.70 ± 6.72 mV, the negatively charged surface helps reduce cytotoxicity and protein adsorption. At the same time, the particle size and zeta potential of CPC/DEX NPs and HCPC/DEX NPs were similar to CPC and HCPC, suggesting DEX was loaded in the inner cavity of β-CD rather than adsorbed on the surface of NPs.

As shown in Fig. 1e, due to the reduction of CDs proportion, the fluorescence intensity of CDs, CP, CPC, CPC/DEX, HCPC, and HCPC/DEX decreased. Fig. 1f shows the characteristic wavelength-dependent emission of HCPC, proving that the fluorescence of HCPC comes from CDs structure.

To confirm the formation of CPC/DEX inclusion complex, DEX was dissolved in ethanol and added in CPC solution or deionized water under stirring at 40 °C. Photographs were obtained under daylight and 365 nm ultraviolet light. As shown in Fig. 1g, DEX was insoluble in deionized water, while soluble in CPC solution revealing the formation of CPC/DEX inclusion complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro properties of HCPC NPs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S4, DEX has the maximum UV–Vis absorption at 240 nm, so 240 nm was selected as the detection wavelength of DEX. Fig. S5 shows that the HPLC method has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5– 500 μg/mL. Fig. S6 shows the good specificity of the HPLC method. The peak time of blank HCPC NPs and DEX are 2.1 min and 9.7 min with good separation. The peak time of DEX and blank NPs remain unchanged in blank NPs + DEX and HCPC/DEX NPs group, indicating that blank NPs do not interfere with the determination of DEX. Tables S1 and S2 show the good precision and average recovery of HPLC method. Therefore, the HPLC method is suitable for DEX detection in HCPC/DEX NPs.

The formulation of HCPC/DEX NPs was optimized by taking DL and EE as indicators. Effects of the mass ratio of CPC/DEX and CPC/HA on NPs were shown in Fig. 2a and b, with the increase of CPC/DEX, DL decreased while EE increased. When the mass ratio of CPC/DEX is 15:1, the increase of EE slowed down while DL decreased rapidly, probably because there is enough CPC to load DEX at 10:1. With the increase of CPC/HA ratio, the DL of NPs increased, when CPC/HA is 1:1, NPs has the highest EE and high DL. So CPC/DEX 10:1 and CPC/HA 1:1 is selected for NPs preparation. As a result, the DL and EE of HCPC/DEX NPs are 4.79 % and 93.31 %, respectively.

The stability of HCPC/DEX NPs was shown in Fig. 2c and d, there are no significant changes in particle size and PDI after incubation at 4 °C, but the PDI of NPs increased after 4 days at 37 °C, suggesting the better stability of HCPC/DEX NPs at 4 °C. Fig. S7 shows the storage stability of lyophilized HCPC/DEX NPs, NPs has better stability storing under 4 °C. Therefore, at room temperature, HCPC/DEX NPs solution should be used within three days, otherwise, they should be lyophilized and stored in the refrigerator. Drug-loaded NPs may dissociate under the action of hemodilution after intravenous injection (Mérian et al., 2015). Fig. S8 shows the good dilution stability of HCPC/DEX NPs, indicating its potential for intravenous administration. Fig. S9 shows the good protein resistance of HCPC/DEX NPs, the negative and hydrophilic surface may reduce the nonspecific binding between NPs and plasma proteins and prolong the circulation time. The stability of DEX in NPs was also determined by detecting DEX concentration in NPs after 24 h of standing. The NPs solution (equivalent to 10 mg of DEX) was stand for 24 h, after that the NPs was disintegrated and the DEX concentration in NPs was detected through HPLC. As shown in Fig. S10, the DEX concentration in CPC/DEX NPs and HCPC/DEX NPs is similar to control group (10 mg of DEX), suggesting the good stability of NPs.

The in vitro drug release was shown in Fig. 2e, a fast and complete drug release is observed in DEX group, 87.6 % of DEX was released within 4 h, in contrast, DEX was released slowly from CPC/DEX and HCPC/DEX NPs. For the first 4 h, only 54.1 % and 24.2 % of DEX were released from CPC/DEX and HCPC/DEX NPs. After 36 h, 85.6 % and 41.2 % of DEX were released from CPC/DEX and HCPC/DEX NPs, indicating both the inclusion action and the cross-linking can slow down the drug release. The environmental responsive drug release was shown in Fig. 2f, the release rate of HCPC/DEX NPs was accelerated in the presence of H2O2 or pH 5.0 conditions. For the first 12 h, 34.5 %, 39.2 %, 36.6 %, and 47.1 % of DEX was released in pH 7.4, pH 7.4 + H2O2, pH 5.0, and pH 5.0 + H2O2 release media, after 72 h, the drug release increased to 49.6 %, 64.5 %, 56.6 %, and 72.5 %. As shown in Fig. 2g, after incubated under pH 5.0 + H2O2 conditions, the HCPC swelled to over 200 nm in 6 h and dissociated in 12 h. Such a phenomenon suggests that the dissociation of HCPC/DEX NPs will be accelerated in the presence of ROS and acidic conditions. Thus, the slow drug release at neutral pH will avoid drug leakage during circulation. The acid and ROS environment in RA joints will promote the DEX release from NPs. At the same time, the TEM images of CPC/DEX incubated under pH 5.0 + H2O2 conditions, no obvious particle size change of NPs can be observed after 12 h, indicating the environmental responsive of HCPC/DEX NPs comes from the ROS and acid degradation of HA. It should be noted that, HA was cross-linked to form nanoparticles. The high viscosity of HA makes the NPs impossible to completely disperse in a short time when they start to dissociate, but form smaller particles or looser aggregates. At the initial stage, a large number of drugs are still distributed in the particles with smaller size, or wrapped by aggregates, which hindered its completely release to the release medium for HPLC detection. Besides, as a hydrophobic drug, DEX also needs a certain time to release from cyclodextrin, so DEX will not be completely released immediately after the dissolution of NPs. As shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity of HCPC remained almost constant after 12 h incubating under pH 5.0 + H2O2 conditions, indicating the good fluorescence property of HCPC. In addition, the drug release increased with the increase of H2O2 concentration (as shown in Fig. 2h), but the drug release almost no longer increase when the H2O2 concentration reached 1 mM, as a result, 1 mM of H2O2 was used for ROS responsive drug release simulating, such a concentration was also used in previous study (M. Chen et al., 2019). Fig. 2i shows that the hemolysis ratio of HCPC is only 2.85 % at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL, revealing the good biocompatibility of HCPC.

The cell viabilities are shown in Fig. 3a and b, the cell viabilities decrease <10 % after incubating with 200 μg/mL of HCPC and HCPC/DEX NPs, and decrease <20 % with 500 μg/mL, suggesting the low cytotoxicity of HCPC and HCPC/DEX NPs.

To study the cell imaging capability of HCPC, cells were induced by LPS to simulate cells at inflammatory sites. For example, RAW 264.7 cells were induced by LPS, and the expression of CD86 was measured via qRT-PCR to confirm the polarization of M1 macrophages, and the LPS induced macrophages were used for cell uptake experiments. As shown in Fig. 3c, the CD86 expressed in LPS induced RAW 264.7 cells is much higher than that in normal RAW 264.7 cells, indicating the polarization of macrophages toward M1 type. As shown in Fig. 3g, after incubating with HCPC, blue, green, and orange fluorescence can be observed in cells under different excitation. The three fluorescence can be colocalized in cells, indicating that HCPC can be taken up and emit wavelength-dependent fluorescence in cells. Fig. 3h shows the cell distribution of HCPC. Caco-2 cells were incubated with HCPC, after that, DAPI was used to stain the nuclei. The green fluorescence from HCPC is mainly observed in the cytoplasm, revealing that HCPC is mainly distributed in the cytoplasm after cell uptaking.

In previous studies, researchers often use single fluorescent method to observe the intracellular process of nano drug delivery platform. Although the drugs or NPs can be labeled for cell imaging, the states of drugs and NPs cannot be revealed at the same time to observe the process of drug release. While the rate of drug release from NPs will significantly affect the therapeutic effect. In this study, CDs fluorescence can effectively show the intracellular process of HCPC NPs, combined with the drug substitute (near-infrared dye IR780), the drug release process of HCPC NPs in cells can be observed through dual fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 3i, the intracellular drug release of HCPC NPs was monitored through cell imaging. LPS induced C28/I2 cells were cultured overnight, after incubating with HCPC/IR780 NPs for 2 h, slight fluorescence appears in cells. After 6 h, abundant fluorescence distributes in cells, and the green and red fluorescence are collocated in cytoplasm, indicating that HCPC/IR780 NPs has been taken up by cells. The red fluorescence gradually separated from green at 12 h, revealing that IR780 begins to release from NPs. The IR780 and HCPC are significantly separated after 24 h, suggesting the massive release of IR780. The above results indicated that HCPC NPs can release drugs within 12 h in cells, which may be related to the dissociation of HCPC NPs in lysosomes.

The HA mediated specific M1 macrophage targeting is shown in Fig. 3j, after incubating with CPC, bright fluorescence can be observed in normal, LPS induced and CD44 blocked macrophages, it could be because the small particle size and positively charged surface of CPC make it easier to be taken up by cells. After incubating with HCPC, the fluorescence in normal and CD44 blocked macrophages is weak, probably because the larger particle size and negatively charged surface of HCPC hinder its cell internalization. While the fluorescence in LPS induced macrophages is much stronger, because of the high expression of CD44 receptors on M1 macrophages, HCPC can be targeted in cells through a receptor-ligand interaction between HA and CD44. Fig. 3k showed the similar result. The above results show that HCPC is mainly taken up by M1 macrophages through the HA-CD44 interaction. It could be noted that, Fig. 3i to k are shown to compare the fluorescence intensity of different groups, so as to study the intracellular drug release and the targeting of NPs. Our previous studies have proven that after staining with CDs, cells will be difficult to be stained with nuclear dyes. This may be due to the fact that CDs have nuclear targeting ability and can emit blue fluorescence, which will influence the effect of nuclear staining. Besides, the cell fixation before DAPI staining will influence the detection result of CDs, therefore, DAPI staining is not conducive to analyzing the cell uptake and targeting effect of NPs. At the same time, the results in this study are able to displayed the cell distribution, revealed the intracellular drug release process, and confirmed the targeting of NPs toward M1 macrophages.

Three typical proinflammatory cytokines were evaluated to assess the effects of HCPC/DEX NPs on LPS induced macrophages. As shown in Fig. 3d to f, blank HCPC NPs has no significant effect on cytokines, free DEX can reduce the levels of three cytokines, the effects of HCPC/DEX NPs are similar to free DEX, and the effects are enhanced in 48 h. It could be because NPs can be targeted to M1 macrophages and responsively dissociated in lysosomes, the enhanced DEX release can effectively reduce the proinflammatory cytokines and inhibit the further macrophage M1 polarization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and therapeutic effects of HCPC NPs
ce:section.ce:para: CFA was intradermally injected into male SD rats to establish AA rats model, and the therapeutic agents were administered intravenously for treatment (as shown in Fig. 4a).

On Day 30, HCPC/IR780 NPs was intravenously injected into normal or AA rats to investigate the in vivo biodistribution. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence signal of free IR780 is almost invisible at 6 h, implying the rapid clearance of IR780. In CPC/IR780 group, the fluorescence disappeared at 6 h, indicating the addition of PEG can prolong the circulation time of IR780. HCPC/IR780 NPs was also injected into AA rats. At 2 h, IR780 is concentrated in the trunk of rats. At 6 h, fluorescence appeared in the inflamed joints. At 24 h, the fluorescence decreased in organs and remained in joints. After injecting HCPC/IR780 NPs in normal rats, fluorescence remains longer than CPC/IR780 group and is mainly concentrated in the trunk of rats, indicating that the NPs targets inflammatory sites rather than joints. According to this feature, HCPC/IR780 NPs can be used to monitor the progress of joint inflammation by observing the accumulation of fluorescence.

To investigate the distribution of NPs in organs, AA rats were sacrificed and the major organs and hind paws were collected for ex vivo imaging. According to Fig. 4c, at 2 h, IR780 distributes in all tissues, especially in lung, liver, and spleen, which is consistent with the in vivo imaging result in Fig. S12. At 6 h, the fluorescence decreased in organs and increased in inflamed joints. At 24 h, the fluorescence in organs disappeared, and that in joints remained. It could be because, after administration, NPs can be passively targeted to inflamed joints, then actively targeted in M1 macrophages to prolong the retention time.

The body weight of rats was recorded, as shown in Fig. 4d. During the experiment, the body weight in each group increased steadily. It was found that HCPC/DEX treatment can cause more weight gain than other treatment groups. Fig. 4e shows the AI of rats after treatment. Compare with AA group, AI of DEX and CPC/DEX groups decreases in the early treatment stage, but the therapeutic effect is gradually weakened after Day 22. Because of the rapid drug release, DEX shows a short residence time and insufficient concentration in inflamed joint to exert a therapeutic effect. HCPC/DEX NPs group shows the best therapeutic effect, the AI decreases to 4.83 ± 0.98 at Day 30. The right hind paw thickness after treatment is shown in Fig. 4f, free DEX and CPC/DEX have a limited effect on paw swelling, while HCPC/DEX NPs has the most significant effect on paw swelling, indicating that NPs is effective in RA treatment.

In inflammatory joints, M1 macrophages increase and secrete excessive proinflammatory cytokines. At the same time, the content of ROS increases, forming a chronic inflammatory environment and triggering a vicious circle of inflammation aggravation and joint injury. Excessive proinflammatory cytokines exist not only in joint synovium but also in blood, which will cause systemic complications. So it is significant to determine the proinflammatory cytokines in synovial tissue and serum. As shown in Fig. 4g to i, compare with the normal group, proinflammatory cytokines increase in AA rats, indicating severe local inflammation. The cytokines decrease in varying degrees after treatment, the cytokines in HCPC/DEX NPs group can be reduced close to normal group. Fig. S13 shows similar results in serum, suggesting HCPC/DEX NPs can effectively inhibit macrophage M1 polarization and show anti-inflammatory effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-CT and histological analysis of the inflamed ankle joints
ce:section.ce:para: Micro-CT and histological analysis were carried out for further evaluation of therapeutic effect in AA rats.

All animals were sacrificed on Day 30 and the hind paw ankle joints were dissected for micro-CT scanning and histological analysis. As shown in Fig. 5 , compared with the normal group, serious subchondral bone sclerosis happens in AA group, the thickness of cartilage decreases, trabecular osteopenia, osteophytes, and serious joint injury occurs. The decrease of BV/TV, Tb.Th, Tb.N, and BMD of distal tibia indicate osteopenia and osteoporosis. Free DEX shows poor effect, the pathological changes such as the decrease of articular cartilage thickness, subchondral bone sclerosis, osteopenia, and osteoporosis can still be observed. It could be due to the short half-life of DEX, and the low drug concentration at lesion location. The destruction of cartilage in CPC/DEX group is alleviated, but there are still joint injuries and osteophytes, and the bone histomorphometry parameters are lower than those in normal group. In HCPC/DEX NPs group, the morphological structure of joint is similar to normal group, the osteophyte, and subchondral bone sclerosis are improved, and the symptoms of osteopenia and osteoporosis decrease. The HCPC/DEX NPs can not only increase the drug concentration in inflamed joints but also inhibit macrophage M1 polarization and reduce ROS production at the inflammatory site to avoid further joint injury. The characteristics of HCPC/DEX NPs targeting M1 macrophages to release drugs may help avoid the DEX from being directly released into the articular cavity to reduce the influence of GCs to bone. As a result, HCPC/DEX NPs shows the best RA therapeutic effect.

The histological examination of ankle joint tissues was carried out by H&E and SOFG staining. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, the joint structure in normal group is complete, the cartilage layer is thick, and there is no inflammatory cell infiltration. In AA group, inflammatory cells such as lymphocytes and macrophages infiltrate, cartilage degenerates, and chondrocytes die. In DEX group, cartilage damage is still obvious, inflammatory cell infiltration and cartilage degeneration are serious. In CPC/DEX group, the thickness of cartilage increases, and the cartilage structure is repaired to a certain extent, but inflammatory cell infiltration is still obvious. In HCPC/DEX group, the joint structure is relatively complete, proteoglycans increase, inflammatory cells decrease significantly, and the thickness of cartilage is similar to normal group. According to Fig. 6c and d, the thickness of cartilage in HCPC/DEX group increases to >400 μm, which is close to normal group. Histological score of inflammatory cell infiltration also shows that HCPC/DEX group has the best RA therapeutic effect.

The above results demonstrate that HCPC/DEX NPs can desirably improve the in vivo therapeutic effect of RA. The remarkably therapeutic effect of HCPC/DEX NPs mainly results from the M1 macrophage targeting, inflammatory microenvironment responsive drug release, and macrophage M1 polarization inhibition in inflamed joints.

To evaluate the safety of HCPC/DEX NPs in vivo, normal rats were injected intravenously with HCPC/DEX NPs for 5 times. Animals were sacrificed and the blood biochemical indexes, organ indexes, and H&E-stained sections of main organs were analyzed.

According to Fig. 7a, compare with control group, the organ tissues in HCPC/DEX NPs group show no substantive lesions or inflammation. As shown in Table S3 and Fig. 7b, there is no significant difference in blood biochemical indexes and organ indexes between HCPC/DEX NPs group and control group, indicating the good biosafety of HCPC/DEX NPs.

In this study, with the help of two carbohydrates, β-CD and HA, the M1 macrophage targeting and environmental responsive drug delivery platform HCPC/DEX NPs was successfully developed. HCPC/DEX NPs can deliver DEX to the inflammation site of RA joints, play an important role in RA treatment by inhibiting inflammatory response in M1 macrophages, and reduce the adverse reactions caused by DEX. The contributions of β-CD, HA, and CDs are confirmed and listed as follows.
(1)
β-CD: the drug delivery platform was constructed by using the extensive and good loading capacity of β-CD to lipophilic drugs or fluorescent substances. The NPs prepared in this study can not only efficiently load DEX (EE is 93.31 %), but also load lipophilic IR780 to form a dual fluorescence imaging system with CDs for dynamic observation of the distribution and drug release process of HCPC NPs in cells.
(2)
HA: HA-based NPs can increase the uptake of M1 macrophages through CD44-mediated endocytosis pathway, and improve the drug targeting of M1 macrophages. This has been confirmed in cell uptake assessment. Moreover, the cross-linking of HA can delay the drug release under physiological pH due to the increase of molecular weight and viscosity, and the generation of steric hindrance, preventing the premature DEX release from NPs. So as to ensure the efficient delivery of DEX to the M1 macrophages and inflammatory sites of RA joints, and play its therapeutic role. In addition, no other reports have been searched that utilize the potential acid and ROS responsive degradation of HA to form RA therapeutic nanodrug for responsive drug release in M1 macrophages.
(3)
CDs: the CDs prepared in this study have good water solubility and fluorescence performance, and the water solubility of β-CD can be improved after linking with CDs to form CPC. Besides, the abundant functional groups on CDs are helpful for further modification with HA to form HCPC/DEX NPs.

Comparing with other nano drug delivery platforms, aside from the features mentioned previously, some further advantages need to be pointed out. First, the main materials of NPs, including β-CD, HA, and CDs, are biocompatible and their safety has been verified. This is very important for RA requiring long-term medication. Moreover, HCPC/DEX NPs is relatively simple in structure, but has multifunctions. In conclusion, this study utilized the characteristics of HA, β-CD, etc. to form a drug delivery platform targeting M1 macrophages at the inflammatory site of RA joints. HCPC/DEX NPs can environmental responsively release DEX and inhibit the inflammatory response in M1 macrophages to enhance the therapeutic effect of RA and reduce the adverse drug reaction. At the same time, a dual fluorescence imaging method can be used for dynamic observation of the drug release process. This study offers a promising platform for RA therapeutic drug delivery.
